29,IssueCommentEvent,2018-01-06 16:39:06+00:00,2918192,epsy/clize,685865,epsy,,,286491551,355758620,"You're calling `run(clize_log_level, ...)` once in each module, therefore arguments get interpreted twice. You can't notice it normally because your function does not product output of its own, but when you use `--help`, then the help is printed twice, because arguments were printed twice.
What you can do is move that call to `run(clize_log_level., ...)` into `config.py`. That way, because Python runs a module's code only once, your log-level setter will only run once.

Note that this is a highly unusual way to structure code for Python. Normally you would call `clize.run(...)` only in the module first executed by Python. You can do that by putting it into an `if __name__ == '__main__'` block [like in the tutorial](http://clize.readthedocs.io/en/stable/basics.html#a-minimal-application).
Your test logging code would go in a function of its own that you'd call after clize, e.g.:
You would do the same in `run_tests.py`, by importing the functions you created and running all three
In the future, you should look to avoid using `exit=False`: if there is an error in processing the parameters, the program would continue anyway.

This means making a way to have both the log-level setter and the ""business"" function be one function together. Fortunately you can pass functions as parameters in Python, e.g. `set_log_level_then_call(first_test, loglevel='DEBUG')`. The log level
You just have to hide this from clize so it can just worry about the loglevel parameter:
Or alternatively: `set_loglevel_then_call_first = functools.partial(set_log_level_then_call, first_test)`, which does the same.
Eventually you'll want to reverse the roles and make set_loglevel [a decorator](http://clize.readthedocs.io/en/stable/compositing.html).
But for now I think you can ignore this last section :)",{},OWNER,User,False

379,IssueCommentEvent,2018-04-28 07:27:05+00:00,38900806,aleju/imgaug,11698516,aleju,,,316148622,385148694,"You say that you are augmenting data in threads. Are you aware that this is to a degree pointless in python, as python threads do not use multiple cores? You have to use background processes to make proper use of multi-core architectures.

None of the augmenters are designed to be thread-safe, precisely because usually processes will be used instead of threads. For normal augmentation this will probably not be a problem, but when using determinism the result becomes undefined.

PiecewiseAffine is a wrapper around around scikit's warp function and PiecewiseAffineTransform. Not sure why these would cause any particular problems in multi-thread scenarios. Your problems sound like each thread is running into an error at some point and for some reason doesn't display that error. Once all threads have crashed, no data is generated any more and your training stalls.

Also, PiecwiseAffine is not a part of any other augmenter. It should not pop up once you comment out the respective line, though its always possible that a combination of other augmenters might end up looking visually similar.",{},OWNER,User,False

439,IssueCommentEvent,2018-01-17 12:29:37+00:00,44054354,dabeaz/curio,350836,dabeaz,,,132204136,358289891,To be fair, I'm allowed to have a sense of humor and the users of a language named after a surreal comedy troupe ought to expect it.

More seriously though, has anyone actually looked at this async world?  It's terrible in just about every respect.  Two categories of functions (sync/async) that can't mix together well.  All these walled gardens of framework code that can't interoperate.  Peril at every turn with unexpected blocking operations.  Callbacks.  Impossible debugging.  I wouldn't wish this environment on an enemy.

Yet, here we are in a world where people hear some thought leader say ""THREAD BAD FOR FACEBOOK"", but they only only hear the ""THREAD BAD"" part.  So, they go around posting ""THREAD BAD"" on Reddit and Hacker News.   Someone posts an animated GIF of a dozen cats trying to chase a squirrel across an ice sheet into their Slack channel with a ""THREADS! LOL!"" meme attached to it. Someone responds with a video of GIL the Golden Eagle pulling the Testing Goat off a cliff, plunging it to its death.  So, you get this whole async cargo-cult of coding, but not much thinking.   Thinking that might eventually involve stunning revelations like ""WAIT! We're not actually Facebook!""

So, no, I'd claim that probably 99% of the people who come here absolutely positively should NOT be using Curio.  Seriously.  They should be using threads.  Use threads and the standard libraries that most other people use to solve problems (requests, Flask, Django, SQLAlchemy, whatever.).    If, for some reason, you find out that you've become Facebook, you're going to have about a million other problems that need to be solved far beyond your choice to use threads.  If the solution to one of those problems actually involves async, then come back and consider Curio.  At that point, the fact that Curio is an async library that doesn't actually claim to do anything might be properly appreciated as a feature and not a limitation.

,{},OWNER,User,False
442,IssueCommentEvent,2018-01-21 04:49:58+00:00,44054354,dabeaz/curio,609896,njsmith,,,288434027,359223876,"Sans-IO"" doesn't usually refer to libraries with interchangeable backends – the prototypical examples like hyper-h2, h11, `ssl.SSLObject` work directly with bytes in memory and literally do not do any I/O at all. We don't have a word for libraries with interchangeable backends... though maybe we should, because the scope and issues are somewhat different. One of the challenges you run into is, yeah, that different libraries are genuinely different in how they handle things – even libraries like trio and curio that share a lot of ideas still have differences all over the place, and not just `TaskError`. (One fun one is that they disagree about which functions should be marked `async`, which can easily leak out to affect your whole API.) I think there is a role for libraries with interchangeable backends, but that it's mostly for libraries that only use the backend in a fairly trivial way. Like [urllib3](https://github.com/njsmith/urllib3/issues/1) seems to working OK, because all it cares about is opening/closing sockets and sending/receiving data on them. But as soon as you get into concurrency etc., I suspect in most cases you're better off picking a single backend and sticking to it. One thing Sans-IO libraries can give you though is to make it surprisingly easy to implement new backend-specific libraries.",{},CONTRIBUTOR,User,False

**TL;DR**: New Will release EOM. Also, you're awesome.

**Full Update:** This may be TMI for you, and if so, cool, the thing you probably care about is above.  But given the total radio silence on my part - and the fact that I've always tried to create a community here where we got to be actual people instead of avatars and PRs, I wanted to give a bit more context.

As you may be aware from an earlier AFK, I got married in December, which was great, but burned the month from a Will perspective.  I planned to get back on it at the start of January, but instead found myself selling our house, and simultaneously hit with a surprisingly severe bout of depression.  The time since Jan 1 has been sort of a blur.  A dark blur, but a blur more than anything.   I saw the PRs and comments coming in, but didn't have the energy or time to do anything about them.
Things seem to be clearing up on both counts now, and I'm _really_ looking forward to getting these fixes in, catching up on bug reports, and being active in improving Will again. :)
With that, I'm aiming to get all outstanding PRs bundled into a release, and all issues replied to by the end of February.

Most of all, thanks for your contribution to Will, your patience with an imperfect, quite human project maintainer, and for continuing to come back, engage, and spend some of the precious seconds you get on earth making this project better.
Thanks for your reply. Totally understand your situation. Will looks like a great tool that can benefit many people. Take it easy man :)

The `loadtime` parameter also used with `lu_sdata` does not need to be passed, however. But yes, everything that the polling loop is doing with shortcodes can be done with full UPnP service variable (aka complex) names.

@pavoni et al, I also spotted something in the Vera Wiki that I have not seen before, and it gave me pause: there's a callout that as of UI7, the `lu_sdata` (and `lu_status`) requests are deprecated, and `status` should be used instead ([here](http://wiki.micasaverde.com/index.php/UI_Notes) under the heading ""user_data"" right before the ""lu_status"" heading). This could be cause for concern for pyvera, as the current subscription code uses `sdata` (short data aka shortcodes). 941,IssueCommentEvent,2017-09-05 12:00:29+00:00,36556137,pmaupin/pdfrw,2379062,pmaupin,,,237855653,327154537,"According to the PDF reference, content streams are mandatory for pages:
> Each page of a document is represented by one or more content streams. Content
streams are also used to package sequences of instructions as self-contained
graphical elements, such as forms (see Section 4.9, “Form XObjects”), patterns

The spec allows (and pdfrw already supports) _empty_ content streams.  But that's different than a _missing_ content stream.  One is zero-length data, and the other is, frankly, a structural file problem.  If you are generating these PDFs with something else, complain to the producer software's author that they should stop making broken PDFs.

Obviously, though, there are so many PDFs out there that are broken in this fashion that the major viewers have thrown in the towel and decided to support them.  I might accept a patch for this, if and only if it was comprehensive and didn't slow down processing for everybody else who is not working with PDFs generated by non-compliant writers.
In any case, though, you can quite easily fix the PDFs in your own pdfrw client code.  For example:

1533,IssueCommentEvent,2016-06-09 18:19:44+00:00,19155959,dagraham/etm-tk,1033127,dagraham,,,158160601,224982159,"Here's a slightly extended version of your example which, I note, is not just a simple sequential list of jobs anymore but has sprouted branches. For the time being, ignore the labels.

As usual, arrows point toward prerequisites so that, for example, getting input from Mary requires that she has been given a header assignment which, in turn, requires that the headers have been prepared. Your example is interesting in that it cannot be represented either as a tree or an outline since the node PPT_HEADERS has multiple parents.

How to represent this structure in etm? Using your @jj and &qq tricks might seem simple to you but would require a complete rewrite of the way etm parses data entries and isn't going to happen. There is, however, a relatively simple alternative. Note that I've attached made-up labels to the left side of each node, e.g., M for Mail, AB for Assign to Bill, RS for Research, etc. The labels don't matter as long as they are unique. Using these labels, I've next attached a list of the prerequisites to the right side of each node. Since Research has no prerequisites it gets an empty list. Summary, on the other hand, gets the list [IB, IM, IR]. You get the idea.  Here would be the corresponding job entries using ""&x"" for the label and &q for the list of prerequisites.

This seems pretty simple to me - even simpler than the number pairs which, of course, would not work in this case anyway. It also allows the assignments to be made in any order and the inputs to be collected in any order, provided only that the relevant assignment has been made. Your @jj entries, on the other hand, suggest that the assignments have to be made in a particular order.

Note that you don't need to construct a graph to do this. Just write down the list of jobs, attach unique labels to each using &x and then, using these labels, add a list of the immediate prerequisites for each job using &q.

1571,IssueCommentEvent,2018-06-26 19:10:35+00:00,67870999,exhuma/puresnmp,17447359,etingof,,,332917668,400429304,"@acspike I think pysnmp 2 is about a decade old, I doubt asyncio even existed back then...
The docs on asyncio interface to pysnmp [are here](http://snmplabs.com/pysnmp/examples/hlapi/asyncio/contents.html). You should probably explain in greater details what puzzles you. ;-)

I guess high-level pysnmp API would always be slower that minimalistic SNMPv2c implementations given that pysnmp implements the whole SNMP system meaning: manager/agent, SNMPv3, proxy, VACM, SMI/MIBs etc.

You may try your luck with [low-level pysnmp API](http://snmplabs.com/pysnmp/examples/contents.html#packet-level-snmp) which operates at roughly similar level as `puresnmp` or `libsnmp` implementations.

However, packet serialization library beneath pysnmp (which is `pyasn1`) is also highly generic, as opposed to implementations, optimized to deal with specific data structures. That may make `pyasn1` somewhat slower than specialized codecs.
But you should really benchmark the fresh versions to get realistic estimation.

1914,IssueCommentEvent,2017-04-23 15:01:00+00:00,3533807,adamreeve/npTDMS,626438,adamreeve,,,223479481,296449289,"Yeah that should be reasonable but there are a couple of things to watch out for. Using multiple threads might not produce any speed up depending on what you're doing, due to the global interpreter lock. If you're mostly calling numpy methods then that should work well as a lot of numpy operations release the GIL. I'd definitely try using multiple threads first and then only try multiple processes if you can't get a speed up using threads. It sounds like there could be some problems with passing the memmapped array into a process using multiprocessing due to issues pickling the array, eg. see http://stackoverflow.com/questions/9964809/numpy-vs-multiprocessing-and-mmap. So you might need to work around that by opening the memmapped file in each of the new processes.",{},,User,False

2153,IssueCommentEvent,2018-05-30 18:28:50+00:00,27896013,moble/quaternion,1470769,moble,,,327468702,393268759,"Yes, as it says in [the docstring for `as_quat_array`](https://github.com/moble/quaternion/blob/5cdfad435b8d97604b7e9a4b900f494535c98f86/__init__.py#L65-L78), the input array must have a final dimension whose size is divisible by four.  The reason is that this function is essentially a wrapper around the numpy `view` method.  So the intent is that you leave data in place (if possible), but just reinterpret it as a series of quaternions.  But quaternions are represented by four consecutive floats, so their corresponding entries in the input array must also be consecutive.  In a C-contiguous array (the most common type), that means that as you increment the index in the last dimension, you move to the next element of the quaternion.  One way or another, memory will have to be copied (which is slow) if your quaternion components are not consecutive.  Moreover, as you also seem to understand, the output from `as_float_array` has the same dimensions and sizes, but an extra axis of size 4 *at the end* of the shape.  So these two functions are basically inverses of each other.

The fact that quaternions are represented as four consecutive floats is absolutely necessary for speed.  There's really no getting around this.  In fact, it's hard coded in `C` — a choice has to be made, and that's the most sensible one for most purposes.  The reason it's the sensible choice is that the vast majority of operations that use quaternions require knowledge of all four components simultaneously.

Which brings me to your proposed operations at the end of your last comment.  That would be the fastest way to accomplish those operations, and I doubt that even a custom-coded `C` function could do it significantly faster for large arrays.  It would also be possible to *add* to the `z` component with something like `quatarray += anotherconst * quaternion.z`.  The only thing I'd caution you about is that you should be sure that you actually want such an operation.  It's unusual to find a useful application of something other than one of the basic algebraic operations on quaternions — addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, exponentiation, logarithm, and closely related things like inversion and square-root.  These are available already.  In fact, there aren't even many useful applications for quaternions apart from rotations, which typically only require the [*group* structure](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Multiplicative_group) of the unit quaternions (multiplication and relatives), as opposed to the full [*algebraic* structure](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Algebra_over_a_field).  I can't think of any good reason to multiply just the `x` and `y` components by some number.",{},OWNER,User,False

2427,IssueCommentEvent,2017-10-14 08:20:32+00:00,90668463,davidhalter/parso,662249,dimaqq,,,265039347,336619473,"`logRecord` (if created) already contains caller's `module` and `pathname`, that is caller frame is already computed for you : https://docs.python.org/2/library/logging.html#logrecord-attributes
Record is created if `logging.root.level` is appropriate (if it might be logged, actual output is up to handlers).
Simply put, `logging.debug()` creates a record if `logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG)` or lower was called.

In the Django-style logging, each module has own logger and that logger has a level set, implicitly or via config. I believe that's done in the name of performance, at the cost of flexibility: configuration is needed in advance, but then e.g. only one sub-sub-module had debug set while the rest of the program doesn't produce records at all.

A Django-based server may provide admin endpoint to change loggers' levels at runtime. So when something's failing, a dev could bump up log levels, run some requests and maybe glean data from live system as to why.

The other way to build large systems is to log a lot, stream all those logs to some aggregation service (in-house ELK or LaaS like splunk, sumo, bigtable), and when system is (or was) failing, a dev searches for logs in the that service dashboard. Because it's designed to support after-the-fact use case, filtering cannot be up-front. Instead, usually there's a different retention policy (e.g. errors are kept for a year, debug for a week).
So, in a sense, we are back to plain-style logging being the norm. I think we are :)",{},NONE,User,False

2667,IssueCommentEvent,2018-07-28 22:52:44+00:00,23058769,nabla-c0d3/sslyze,1915165,nabla-c0d3,,,344875729,408639822,"Haha from my perspective that's actually the perfect example as to why it's better to build your own library:
* Nassl will work 100% the same regardless of what version of OpenSSL is on the system; all the features will work, no surprises.
* Cryptography on the other hand uses the system's OpenSSL which directly led to the bug we're talking about here: different features depending on the version of OpenSSL on the system.

Of course I am not saying shared libraries should never be used, but in the case of ""advanced""/new features of OpenSSL, it's great that nassl does not use them. 99% of users (including myself) do not care about what version of OpenSSL is on their system, and changing it is always a time-consuming ordeal.",{},OWNER,User,False

2671,IssueCommentEvent,2018-06-20 01:34:30+00:00,23058769,nabla-c0d3/sslyze,1915165,nabla-c0d3,,,108320255,398596117,"That's one of the problems with using the systems libraries. Nassl needs to be compiled with both exact versions of OpenSSL: 1.0.2e and 1.1.1 beta. Any other version will not work, and both are needed.
All in all I have no plans to support building with system libraries - it is too unlikely to even work due to how different nassl's use case is.",{},OWNER,User,False

- find the item whose _r:id_ attribute matches the previous reference

3430,IssueCommentEvent,2017-02-01 22:27:25+00:00,77753230,bstriner/keras-tqdm,2578089,rohitrawat,,,204159850,276803468,"On looking at [keras/engine/training.py#L1483](https://github.com/fchollet/keras/blob/c07d0e6448bb63762bc7a19d87814f6fba79fa32/keras/engine/training.py#L1483), `'batch_size'` is not included in `params` when it is built by `fit_generator()`

On the other hand, `_fit_loop()` which is called by `fit()` ([keras/engine/training.py#L855](https://github.com/fchollet/keras/blob/c07d0e6448bb63762bc7a19d87814f6fba79fa32/keras/engine/training.py#L855)) does define the `'batch_size'`.

It turns out that `fit_generator()` does not know the batch size as it is determined by however many samples are enqueued by the `generator`. This may be a fixed known number for certain types of generators, but not for others. This prevents us from having an inner progress bar like we have for `fit()`.

The default Keras `ProgbarLogger` does not attempt to show the progress of batches within each epoch (there is no inner progress bar), thus is not affected by the missing `'batch_size'`. A fix on similar lines will turn off the inner progress bar when `'batch_size'` key is missing.

3631,IssueCommentEvent,2016-11-07 22:24:50+00:00,29050338,mementum/backtrader,5713511,mementum,,,187644069,258983002,"Indeed a sample would help. There is no reason for the platform to use *price* with a value of `0` for a execution.
The broker simulation checks submitted orders to understand if they would push the cash level below `0` (ie: it doesn't admit leverage, because the cash must be in the account)
If you `close` a position, the pseudo-execution will say that you have enough cash after the fact and the order will be allowed in the system.

If the order is not allowed it will be rejected with status `Order.Margin`. Of course if you check `order.executed.price` after such a status notification, it will be `0.0` (`None` would have also been an option), because the order has *not* been executed.",{},,User,False

* I've added a method to handle large result sets using the raw requests callback which can then be added into an lxml iterable reader implementation (which I am now coding out).

Using a `$viewChangeListener` to remove the 'rejected' error means that the error will only be removed if the input is interacted with. But not if the model data is changed from somewhere else.

If you look at the console output, you'll see that the `$viewChangeListener` for 'maybe' has not be fired and you still need to select all 3 radio buttons individually for the 'rejected' error to be removed from the `ngForm`.

Instead of adding a `$viewChangeListener` in `djangoForm.setErrors` to remove the 'rejected' error, we add a directive to each input that handles the error in the same way as the other angular validators i.e required, min length, maxlength etc.
<input name=""cheese"" ng-model=""data.cheese"" required djng-rejected type=""radio"" value=""no"">
if you follow the same steps as before you'll see that the 'rejected' error is now cleared

Due to the way in which `ngModel` `$formatters` and `$parsers` work, the new `djng-rejected` directive has also solved the previous problem, of clearing all rejected errors when a single radio button is selected. As they are all bound to the same model value on scope - `data.cheese` - if any of them are selected, their error is cleared by their `$parsers` being called and it then automatically fires the `$formatters` of the other 2, clearing their rejected error and removing the 'rejected' error from the `ngForm`.
We've now completely removed the need for the `validate-multiple-values` directive on a `RadioSelect`.",{},,User,False

4565,IssueCommentEvent,2017-12-26 19:32:18+00:00,30091395,rnelsonchem/simpledbf,3750670,rnelsonchem,,,174928862,354004615,"My guess is that the assertion errors are a result of incorrect DBF file versions. There is a section in the README that explains which DBF versions are acceptable.

Have a look at the table entitled ""Database file header"" on the [dbf Wikipedia page](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/.dbf). The very last byte of the header should be `0x0D`, which is is a [carriage return](http://www.bluesock.org/~willg/dev/ascii.html). If that is not true, then you might have a different file version. For example in [DBF version 7](http://www.dbase.com/Knowledgebase/INT/db7_file_fmt.htm), the header has a much different length, so this code will not process it correctly.
You can check the DBF version info from the first byte of your file as follows:

[Convert](https://www.binaryhexconverter.com/hex-to-binary-converter) this hex value into binary. As per the [DBF7](http://www.dbase.com/Knowledgebase/INT/db7_file_fmt.htm) description, bits 0-2 tell you about the version number. For example, I downloaded the file ""Health Indicator Attributes"" from the [HRSA website](https://datawarehouse.hrsa.gov/data/datadownload/pcsa2010Download.aspx). When I checked the first byte, I got `'\x03'`, which converts to `0000 0011` in binary. The first three bits (on the far right, i.e. 011) are equivalent to 3, which according the DBF7 notes linked above indicates a DBF version 5 file.

I hope this helps. If you'd like, you can use this code as a template to create a DBF version 7 reader using the format link that I've provided. I only deal with older DBF versions, so I won't have any bandwidth to make updates to this project.",{},OWNER,User,False

4647,IssueCommentEvent,2016-12-24 17:09:33+00:00,30582779,warner/magic-wormhole,27146,warner,,,158480996,269092310,"Updated the title: the real feature here is that we should survive losing the connection to the Rendezvous server, by re-establishing it and then re-sending whatever we needed to send. At the moment I'm very hesitant to upgrade or reboot the Rendezvous server because it would interrupt any half-opened wormholes in progress. With this change, that should no longer be a problem: as long as the server isn't down for more than 10 or 20 minutes, the clients should be able to sync back up properly. (if it's down for longer than 20 minutes, the server might start pruning the mailboxes for lack-of-use, but it only takes a minute to reboot).

The *Transit* server is another matter, and if it bounces, any relay-using transfers will fail. #88 could cover that, although #88 is currently about human-retriggered retries rather than automatic retries, which would be nice.

4660,IssueCommentEvent,2017-02-02 20:14:09+00:00,30582779,warner/magic-wormhole,27146,warner,,,158540018,277069961,"> How are channels allocated on the rendezvous server? Just pick the lowest available one?

Pretty close. The [allocation code](https://github.com/warner/magic-wormhole/blob/0.9.2/src/wormhole/server/rendezvous.py#L179) chooses a random single-digit code, if any are available. If not, it chooses a random two-digit code. If those are all used up, it does the same for three-digit codes. If *those* are used up too, it just starts picking randomly from 1000-1000000 for a thousand trials and hopes one of them is free.
I wanted to make it slightly more random than ""always lowest available"", so that people didn't get into the habit of assuming that codes always started with `1-`.
> Could you just use a random function to pick any MDNS channel from 0-20?

Yeah, that would probably work. (we treat ""0"" as special, for an unauthenticated no-code-necessary throw-caution-to-the-wind mode). If we know that we're being asked to allocate a code (e.g. `wormhole send` without the explicit `--code=` argument), then it'd be nice to use MDNS to query for which channels are already in use, to reduce the chance of collisions somewhat (not entirely, because of races). But if the range we're picking from is large enough, and the rate at which people are using them is low enough (on a given broadcast domain), then it probably doesn't matter.
> In any case, it would be trivially easy for somebody to just DoS the broadcast domain and force a fallback to the rendezvous server by squatting all service names.
The same DoS attack is easy to do against the rendezvous server, so no real change there.

,{},,User,False

4689,IssueCommentEvent,2018-06-06 18:41:34+00:00,17410607,GeospatialPython/pyshp,6413369,karimbahgat,,,288586460,395172280,"Thanks for this, and apologies for the late reply (#145). I agree that retrieving record values in real-world scenarios is not currently the most intuitive, so a new Record class sure could be a useful addition. I might be open to this idea, but there are some issues with the PR that I'm not yet sure how I feel about:

- I think this could be rewritten with only the bare necessities, least amount of changes/new classes/new args/possible breaks. I would prefer this rewritten as just a Record class, initiated using the row values and the shapefile fields list directly. No namedtuples, no RecordFactory. Just a Record class with a fields list and a value list as args, that should be sufficient to retrieve record values based on field names, the main point of the PR.

- Not sure I see the point of storing a record 'oid' (which scenarious would this be useful?), it might add more complexity that it's worth (eg would it break if the sequence of rows are changed, what about writing records, etc). But would be happy to hear arguments why it would be a good idea.

- While I agree that accessing the values as attributes is certainly easier than with name indexing, this might prove problematic. Since the Record() class is supposed to subclass 'list' for compatibility, this would mean that when trying to accesss any field names with the same name as a list property or method ('index', 'append', etc), one would get those internal values or methods instead of the record value. This might prove confusing and mask the real problem for some users. But maybe it's an acceptable problem, I would be curious to hear if other libs use this approach and how they handle it (eg I wonder if Pandas uses this type of row value access?).
- The PR also duplicated a previous PR and now has a conflict.

I was considering implementing the simpler version of this, but was curious about some of these issues, and also need some more time to think/discuss if there might be some scenarios that would create compatibility problems or if handling the Record class might prove less intuitive than just a simple list.

5043,IssueCommentEvent,2018-02-07 11:01:47+00:00,4742675,jbittel/django-mama-cas,463832,manelclos,,,294732933,363734367,"The cookies are used to check that you have a valid session with every app. So you have a cookie for the CAS app, a different one for App1, etc.

When you try to access App1 for the first time, there is no session cookie for App1. As you have configured App1 to use the CAS, you are redirected to the CAS app. First time there is also no cookie for the CAS app, so it asks you for credentials (let's say we are using username/password), and once you authenticate, it redirects you back to App1 with the ticket. That ticket is read by the CAS CLIENT and using an http request it is checked against the CAS SERVER. If the ticket is valid, the CAS client will log you in App1, which will produce the ""side effect"" of setting the session cookie for App1.

So next time you access App1, as you are already logged in, there is no need to check against the CAS app. Checking against the CAS app will only happen when the session cookie for App1 expires or you log out from App1.

When login into App2, you are redirected to the CAS app, as the session cookie for the CAS app is still valid (has not expired), you are NOT asked for credentials, you are ALREADY logged into the CAS app, so you get a redirect back to App2 with a new ticket that App2 will check. App2 will then log you in and set a cookie for App2.

If at this point you log out from the CAS app, session cookies for App1 and App2 are still valid, you can continue to use them, but if you try to login into App3, the CAS app will ask for credentials.

If you logout from App1, and you have FULL logout enabled, App1 CAS CLIENT will send a logout request to the CAS app, and in turn the CAS app will send logout requests to the other Apps (App2, App3 ...).

Watch the cookies as they are created, and play with deleting them and accessing apps or cas again. Along the ""preserve log"" option to see redirects, it will give you the full picture of what is going on.

6159,IssueCommentEvent,2015-11-28 18:22:38+00:00,15157083,jrief/django-websocket-redis,7114869,malefice,,,39185319,160327373,Confirmed! Using the requirements listed in a [previous post](https://github.com/jrief/django-websocket-redis/issues/47#issuecomment-148168524) works if you use the `./manage.py runserver` to test, and so far it works great with Python 3.

I encountered some issues though when attempting to use uwsgi as a standalone server as proposed in this [section of the docs](https://django-websocket-redis.readthedocs.org/en/latest/running.html?highlight=master#django-with-websockets-for-redis-as-a-stand-alone-uwsgi-server). After several print statements, I found out that the `Websocket` object used when using the uwsgi command is different from the one used by `./manage.py runserver`.

Long story short, bytestrings are being passed into the `RedisMessage` wrapper at this [specific line](https://github.com/jrief/django-websocket-redis/blob/master/ws4redis/wsgi_server.py#L105), but the `RedisMessage` wrapper only handles `str` or `list` types causing all websocket messages sent to be discarded.
I wrote PR [#122](https://github.com/jrief/django-websocket-redis/pull/122) to address that, and so far, everything is working.",{},,User,False

6289,IssueCommentEvent,2015-09-30 15:50:45+00:00,36301659,st4lk/django-rest-social-auth,7449642,skolsuper,,,108659696,144454942,"Just to be clear, the client flow is:
1. empty POST request to our view, receive request token
2. GET to Twitter to authenticate, receive oauth_verifier
3. POST the oauth_verifier back to us.
2. GET a fresh request token from Twitter with our consumer secret and return it to the client
3. Receive the oauth_verifier from the client in another POST request
4. Use that to authenticate the user with Twitter, grab their details and create/retrieve their account with us.
5. Send them their token for future requests, optionally along with their user resource.",{},,User,False

6363,IssueCommentEvent,2017-01-31 04:26:16+00:00,39119843,miguelgrinberg/python-socketio,4266679,etseidler,,,198604594,276272099,"i completely agree with your assessment of the first potential problem. when i first started adapting the zmq manager, i struggled with this issue for quite a while. the no block flag was not ideal, but it at least provided a way forward. thank you so much for mentioning evenlet's green wrapper for zmq! i used that in place of vanilla zmq and it worked like a charm. i pushed a commit for that change.

yes, i'm using python > 2.7.9, so hopefully i can make any necessary changes to be compatible with python 3. was it the use of str in [this line](https://github.com/miguelgrinberg/python-socketio/pull/64/commits/88f3b87efa9f91d1e6eb23a110962d8d664ff1c3#diff-92b0b81caf2cf586f90ab5f5a5afa89bR104) in particular, or something else?

finally, regarding the message broker that i put together... there is [one project here](https://github.com/zeromq/malamute) that may be in line with what you're looking for. i don't know much about it, but it seems like it may be overkill, at least it would be for my app.

furthermore, while i am no expert on zmq, everything that i have read suggests that zmq eliminates the need for a central broker and if you do happen to need one, you can write your own without too much trouble. see [common zmq patterns](http://learning-0mq-with-pyzmq.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pyzmq/patterns/patterns.html), [broker vs brokerless](http://zeromq.org/whitepapers:brokerless), and [messaging patterns](http://zguide.zeromq.org/page:all#Messaging-Patterns), for example.

admittedly, i don't know the needs of other applications, but i would ask you what specifically is lacking in the simple message repeater/broker? is it performance concerns? custom behavior? architectural flaw? then, at some point, perhaps making a robust zmq message broker becomes a concern that is outside the scope of both flask-socketio and python-socketio. that is, any zmq message broker used would be a necessary part of the system, but doesn't seem to belong in either of the aforementioned repos/projects.
thank you once again for your feedback.",{},,User,False

6894,IssueCommentEvent,2018-05-29 12:34:12+00:00,34864402,apache/incubator-mxnet,5178350,sxjscience,47359,apache,325595530,392759706,"Sounds good. We can add a argument called ‘idx_dtype’, which is set to be backward-compatible by default.
Subject: Re: [apache/incubator-mxnet] Use dtype=int for the indices returned by TopK (#11031)
A few general remarks about indices in mxnet:

There is no specific convention in MXNet about what datatype to be used when encoding indices. It is basically left to the user or inferred from other data. This is generally fine and consistent. For example look at all operators that are indeed consumers of index arrays (pick, take, gather_nd):

They are all written in a way such that the datatype of the indices can be anything, as long as it can be cast to an int internally. In particular, the datatype does not have to be compliant with other inputs to such operators (i.e. take() will accept the data array to be of type double and the index array of type int/float/half_t/whatever). I think this is a really good design and we should not impose any restrictions that the incoming indices must be of integral type. Neither that every index should be integral.

The situation is slightly different for operators that actually produce index arrays (without having any index array as input). Such operators are topk, argsort, argmax, argmin, argmax_channel (hope I didn't forget one here). Currently they infer the index type (used in the output array of the operator) from the datatype used in the input. But that is weird as there is no objective correlation between data type and index type. If data input is float, why should be then the index type also a float? Moreover it can cause weird problems: If you want to get the index of the maximum element of a 10M large array of floats, then the float-type used as output would not be able to represent all potential indices (while an int would be able). To make such thing work, you currently would have to cast the entire data input to doubles, then receive indices as a double, then cast back to int. And even that may not work as the operator may not support doubles (which is the case for topk).

So my proposal would be to make a change for all index-producing operators (listed above) to allow explicit setting of the output index type by a parameter. In order to keep backward compatibility, the default should stay as is (inferred from data etc).

7094,IssueCommentEvent,2016-09-07 15:46:23+00:00,20313056,Yelp/bravado,6726359,macisamuele,49071,Yelp,156563813,245324390,"@necrolyte2 thanks for having provided the Gist ... it helped me a lot to understand what was your point.
The exception that you're getting is related to the fact that you have not correctly specified the parameters.

On [line 130](https://gist.github.com/necrolyte2/e4f8f4f76dd3c5637af8e6cf77a73ca5#file-test-py-L130) you're trying to inject the headers in the requests, but those will be added directly on the request object which is not checked against the specs, so you get ``SwaggerMappingError``.
If you try instead to insert the header parameters like you did for ``body`` and ``key`` you will successfully run your request.
**headers  # dictionary expansion for injecting the header from the dictionary
### More general solution (what I prefer more)
Your code modification will achieve the goal of removing the annoying exception but basically not validate the presence of the required headers parameters.
I would do prefer something like (in case the parameter is a required one):
- check if the parameter is an header parameter
- if so check that is present and compliant with the specs
So you'll enforce also the correct validation of the values injected in the request through ``_request_options``

Checking your specs I have seen that you're using ``security`` specs but your specs are not defining any ``securityDefinitions`` and it is not compliant with the [Swagger Specs](swagger.io/specification/#securityRequirementObject).",{},,User,False

7198,IssueCommentEvent,2017-12-22 07:29:32+00:00,6930489,facebook/watchman,117777,wez,69631,facebook,284090832,353539459,"I don't believe that this behavior changed.  The model for the watchman service is that we run one server process per user.  That server is responsible for handling all watches initiated by that user.  We look at the %USERNAME% environmental variable to figure out who you are and then set up a named pipe.

What I suspect happened is that you launched the first instance after upgrading as Administrator and that prevented non-Administrator sessions from accessing the named pipe created by the administrator.
I'd recommend running `watchman shutdown-server` as Administrator to make the service stop, then run `watchman version` as non-admin to start it up again without privileges.

,{},CONTRIBUTOR,User,False

7766,IssueCommentEvent,2017-03-01 23:28:21+00:00,5888353,spotify/luigi,362534,chrispalmer,251374,spotify,210361886,283505738,Luigi's model of task execution and completeness is primarily based on the logic of:
`A task is complete if and only if it's output exists.`
Some dependency graphs can include hundreds or thousands of tasks, and checking them for completeness is a non trivial process. Repeating all those checks time and again could get very costly.
I think the first question is why did you delete the output from A? In a production environment assuming that A, B and C ran correctly there shouldn't be a need to delete the output.

However if you are still developing a pipeline (or your production tasks are broken), then yes there can be situations where you will find that A didn't run correctly and thus the output from A, B and C is incorrect. That can certainly be annoying to deal with but the only real solution with standard Luigi is to just delete the output from all three, and then run it again.
Beyond that you could write your own custom Task subclass and overwrite the `complete()` method to handle whatever conditions you want.

7992,IssueCommentEvent,2016-10-25 22:58:30+00:00,24774658,boto/boto3,2643092,JordonPhillips,327752,boto,185191861,256202912,"Yes and no. We don't upload it to different keys (and I don't think that's the direction we want to take this feature), but you can still download parts.

If you want to download only a part of the file, you can use `Range` when you perform the `get_object` request or other download. This follows the http [range header](https://www.w3.org/Protocols/rfc2616/rfc2616-sec14.html#sec14.35) semantics, so to get the first 500 bytes you would use `0-500`.

You can also use `PartNumber`, so downloading part 1 would download the first part that was uploaded. Part size is determined by the configuration you pass in, but will be auto-adjusted if your part size is invalid. In this way it's not quite as reliable since you can't be sure how big each part is, but it could be faster and easier to understand.",{},,User,False

Actually, I believe the default for `pipenv install` is `--skip-lock`. In my experience `pipenv install` will change the lock file if it detects an old dependency, as will `pipenv sync`. The only way to get it to use the lock file without modifying it is `pipenv install --ignore-pipfile` (*note: I'm using pipenv version 10.1.0)*

8563,IssueCommentEvent,2018-05-16 08:07:53+00:00,79510167,pypa/pipenv,605277,uranusjr,647025,pypa,256648514,389432700,"> It seems pipenv is not a substitute for pip, but must be used alongside pip.

This is, and will always be the case. Pipenv and pip are more like Gir porcelain and plumbing commands; the former provide a nice interface, but cannot possibly covert all use cases (because abstractions are always leaky), and in edge cases you need to do some plumbing yourself.

With that said, it could be a good idea to add a flag to `clean` to not only clean up unused packages, but also reinstalled others. Or maybe sync can have a flag to pass the `ignore-installed` to pip.",{},COLLABORATOR,User,False

8599,IssueCommentEvent,2016-03-31 23:56:46+00:00,49615802,pypa/auditwheel,609896,njsmith,647025,pypa,144790520,204179577,"Oh god damn it. This is a bug in patchelf: it turns out that when you link against a library like `libgfortran` that uses symbol versioning, then the library gets listed in two different places: once as a DT_NEEDED, and once in the symbol version requirements table. `patchelf --replace-needed` fixes the DT_NEEDED entry, but doesn't touch the symbol version requirements table, so we have:
Dynamic section at offset 0x2318100 contains 28 entries:

Notice that the first part references `libgfortran-ed201abd.so.3.0.0`, but the second part references `libgfortran.so.3`. And then this freaks out the dynamic loader, because it's being asked to check the symbol versions of a library that wasn't even loaded.",{},,User,False

As part of an effort to ease the contribution process and adopt a more standard workflow pip has switched to doing development on the ``master`` branch. However, this Pull Request was made against the ``develop`` branch so it will need to be resubmitted against ``master``. Unfortunately, this pull request does not cleanly merge against the current ``master`` branch.
If you do nothing, this Pull Request will be automatically closed by @BrownTruck since it cannot be merged.

If this pull request is still valid, please rebase it against ``master`` (or merge ``master`` into it) and resubmit it against the ``master`` branch, closing and referencing the original Pull Request.
If you choose to rebase/merge and resubmit this Pull Request, here is an example message that you can copy and paste:

When running the virtualenv.py script located in a dir containing spaces in its path, or when specifying an '--extra-search-dir' path containing spaces, virtualenv would construct invalid findlinks parameter value for pip. This parameter expects to contain a space separated list and so does not allow individual items contained in it to contain spaces. The issue has been fixed by not passing it direct paths for dirs containing spaces. Such dirs are now represented by local 'file://' URLs, URL quoted so they no longer contain spaces, i.e. with space characters converted to '%20'.

The new code could be refactored further and used generic utility functions extracted into some more general utility module, but that, if done, should really be done by or under guidance by someone more familiar with the project's organization than I am.

8817,IssueCommentEvent,2015-06-04 17:13:47+00:00,4978045,Parsely/pykafka,136574,kbourgoin,779641,Parsely,84794731,108979125,"Took a quick look at the implementation and a few things come to mind:

1. You need to monitor thread death. If we're using `threading` (since that's the only handler supported right now) the thread will die silently and you'll just stop producing messages. This is usually solved by creating a registry of the threads running and having a monitor thread which will check their state every so often. Then it can either restart the dead thread or raise the error to the main thread (a tricky thing in its own right).

2. The subclass is very heavily dependent on the implementation of the main class. This isn't technically wrong, but it makes following the implementation of the async producer that much harder.  I'll take another look to puzzle it out, but it feels like a case where trying to avoid duplicated/similar code has made something much more confusing than it needs to be.

3. How does this respond to leaders changing?  If you've got a queue per broker and the broker is no longer correct, will it be shuffled to the right now?  This could be related to point 2, where I don't think I fully understand everything that's going on.",{},,User,False

CondaVerificationError: The package for py located at .../miniconda3/pkgs/py-1.5.3-py36_0
appears to be corrupted. The path 'lib/python3.6/site-packages/py/_code/__pycache__/_assertionold.cpython-36.pyc'
specified in the package manifest cannot be found.
Means you have a corrupt package in your package cache.  Use `conda clean --packages`, and if that doesn't work, just remove it manually.
ClobberError: This transaction has incompatible packages due to a shared path.

Means that conda is alerting you to packages that have overlapping paths, that will very likely become problematic at some later point.  The behavior of conda here is configured via the `path_conflict` configuration parameter.  The parameter can have values of `clobber`, `warn`, or `prevent`.  Currently, because we know we have several problems in `defaults` channels, the default value is `clobber`.  So I assume `conda config --show-sources` for this user will show that the configuration parameter has been changed to `warn` or `prevent`.",{},NONE,User,False

9006,IssueCommentEvent,2015-07-24 00:53:26+00:00,2014929,scikit-image/scikit-image,492549,jni,897180,scikit-image,96707277,124283069,"Oh. Well then I'm 100% against this. =P ""relabeling"" means different things in different contexts. Often I want the behaviour of `ndi.label`, which treats the volume as boolean, and other times I want that of `measure.label`, which looks for contiguous segments of the same label. If we normalize to `ndi.label`, we lose the ability to remove small objects labeled with `measure.label`, and if we normalize to `measure.label`, we lose support for 4D+ images. Also, the current behaviour allows you to maintain your original labels, which can be important in many applications.
In short, I think it's a terrible idea.",{},,User,False

9014,IssueCommentEvent,2018-08-07 05:44:45+00:00,2014929,scikit-image/scikit-image,90008,hmaarrfk,897180,scikit-image,220605068,410940178,"I'm not sure what value we can add by having yet an other custom way of handling metadata. I'm of the mindset that we should not create an other class/collection of objects that acts like `Novice` (even if you call it [`meta`](https://mk0slamonlinensgt39k.kinstacdn.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/02/MWP-MARQUEE.jpg)). Without a clear focus, we simply won't maintain it.

I also want to make sure we also don't get stuck on the ""color conversion"" issue too much. While it is an important issue, we should be able to TRUST the user of our library and not hand hold them too much. I agree it is the first issue people run into when they open their images for the first time, but it quickly becomes a trivial issue in my mind when users learn that their processing can probably be done on the gray image (or maybe just the red channel :dog: ).

Keep in mind that many functions in the color conv module are limited to 10 lines of code (but 30 lines of documentation). I'm willing to bet that many users probably wrote them themselves before finding scikit-image.

Finally datatype, quantization, rounding errors, vectorization are things you MUST learn (slowly) when doing data analysis. We can't shield users from that. What do we do when the user mangles the metadata dictionary and opens an issue?

Most functions only need one or two keyword arguments regarding the image's metadata. Asking a user to buy into a whole OO system is pretty costly. It would have probably turned me away from scikit-image. That is basically what turned me away from PIL's opaque images.

I've come to use `xarray` quite a bit. That said, the real value is that it allows me to keep track of my experimental data and acts more like a digital notebook that can be shared with others. It isn't meant for me to ""magically find the right axis where `hsv` is stored"". To teach users about metadata mangement, we can point them to other libraries we find useful. If other strategies fall in line with the [array protocol](http://www.numpy.org/neps/nep-0018-array-function-protocol.html), then maybe scikit-image doesn't have to do much to accommodate users that want to use fancy numpy arrays.

At the end of the day, I think the power of scikit-image will be to position itself as a powerful library for image processing, as opposed to metadata handling. I am of the mind that documentation and keywords like `axis` to find where the `rgb` axis is, are basically all we need for now.

Regarding the strange warnings, I think those would be best addressed by raising an error. I was bit by the `rank` filters myself. I disagree with the ""transparent"" approach taken there. For those particular functions, the conversion doesn't gain any speed over `img_as_float(rank.the_filter(img_as_byte(my_float_image)))` and therefore I don't see a strong case for implicit type conversion.

,{},MEMBER,User,False

- Current ``maint-2.0`` behaviour is that only public records are exposed via OAI-PMH independent of if you are logged in or not.

Fair question. It's got to do with our target audience; they are not developers, but rather artists with scripting experience. We can't rely on pip, anaconda or any other packaging mechanism, when the lowest common denominator is ""copy/paste this file"". Support for package managers is there, but optional.
> It seems a bit unmanageable in the long run ;-)
I can see why you'd think that. :) In this case we expect it to go both ways - sticking to one file forces us to keep this one file short and maintainable, and keeps us honest about goal 3.
> Why? PySide2 seems to not be ready for the prime time :-)

It's a gamble. We're expecting PySide2 to become the new sorcerer supreme in the not too distant future, and we'd like to support that effort. We expect it to correlate better with the C++ version of Qt in the long run, simply due to its relation to The Qt Company.
But again, it's a gamble and as of now it can go one way or the other. For all we know, the project can fail completely, and at that point we'd have to adapt.
> I think we should join efforts and work on a single shim package that would work really well for both PySide and PyQt.

I admire your optimism! However, as we've [already](https://github.com/mottosso/Qt.py/pull/107) [experienced](https://github.com/mottosso/Qt.py/pull/96), there are a few conflicting requirements neither of us are able to fulfil in one offering - one being the target audience requirement, as mentioned above - another being whether or not to provide ad-hoc implementations to differences across bindings - a third one being; which should be our reference binding?
I think we will do better catering to these different extremes - learning from each other, sharing what we can.
@goanpeca Let me know if the above doesn't answer your questions as well and I'll fill it in!

9976,IssueCommentEvent,2015-02-24 21:26:24+00:00,3510804,GalSim-developers/GalSim,623887,rmjarvis,1462196,GalSim-developers,55228552,75849597,"I'm back to working on this branch now, and I have some results to report about how things work currently.  I pushed an initial version of a test, which doesn't actually have any asserts yet.  It just prints some values so I can see what is going on.  cf. a568dd2

The basic idea is that I take a cosmos noise object, shear it to exaggerate the correlations, and then use it to add noise to images with different WCS functions.  Then I use the CorrelatedNoise class to measure the resulting correlations in the image and compare to the original.
The different wcs functions I use are:
- ""same"" is the same as the original
- ""rot"" has the same size pixels, but rotated by 90 degrees
- ""raw"" means I reset the wcs back to the original pixel scale before measuring the correlations.
Then I look at the correlations in the 3x3 grid around the origin in the resulting measured correlation function.  Here is the output:
orig    same     big    small    rot   big/raw sm/raw  rot/raw

So basically, when we measure the noise using the same wcs as was used to draw the noise, we get back to essentially the original values.  The exception is ""big"", which must be losing too much information by being rendered at effectively wider spacing, so it can't recover the information correctly at the original pixel scale.  I think all that makes sense, although it is possibly not what we want.
I believe Barney's suggestion is that people would instead typically want the raw results to match up with orig, which they currently do not.

I can pretty easily code this up.  The only question is do we care about preserving the current functionality where the code respects the two WCS functions?  Or should we always have `image.addNoise(correlated_noise)` ignore the WCS intrinsic to the noise object and just draw it according to the pixel correlations that were in the original image?  I'm kind of thinking the latter, in which case we won't need the `ignore_wcs` flag, but I'm open to other opinions.",{},,User,False

`neurom.viewer` depends on `view.(neuron, tree, soma neuron3d, tree3d, soma3d)`,
and `view.neuron` depends on `view.soma and view.tree`, and `view.neuron3d` on `view.tree3d, view.soma3d`.
Each of these then call plot_style, which then calls save_plot, which means that save_plot is called several times per view.neuron if output_path exists.
Since the view.* functions are all `Developer API`, I think it's best if:
1. it's up to the user to call `save_plot`
2. it's up to the user to call `plot_style`
3. `neurom.viewer` handles `save_plot` and `plot_style` to keep the public API the same.
This way, a develop has control over when the plot is styled (if it's necessary to use plot_style), and when it's saved, without repeated calls.
This would also include whether a plot is shown, so we wouldn't have to use the AGG backend.",{},,User,False

In the documentation you recommend to use tmux as the default interpreter, but it is not in the combo box list.
The problem is that prefs.py only reads /etc/shells and add some extra values (ipython, python and so), using a regex:
# A regular expression to match possible python interpreters when
# filling interpreters combo in preferences (including bpython and ipython)
# Path to the shells file, it will be used to start to populate
# interpreters combo, see the next variable, its important to fill the
# string to show in prefereces dialog for user shell option

So tmux is not in the shells list  combo for this reason. I fixed mine modifying the regex, but it will be better if this could be properly fixed for the next releases, because some users may like to use ruby or some other.

From my understanding, you are attempting to invoke an endpoint for a XGBoost model that was trained using the SparkSDK. As long as your inference data is serialized to the correct format, then you should be able to invoke the endpoint regardless of the SDK.

For details on serialization, please refer to the serializer defined in each corresponding estimator, https://github.com/aws/sagemaker-spark/blob/master/sagemaker-spark-sdk/src/main/scala/com/amazonaws/services/sagemaker/sparksdk/algorithms/XGBoostSageMakerEstimator.scala#L479.

For XGBoost, the default serializer is for LIBSVM, https://github.com/aws/sagemaker-spark/blob/master/sagemaker-spark-sdk/src/main/scala/com/amazonaws/services/sagemaker/sparksdk/transformation/serializers/LibSVMRequestRowSerializer.scala.
This would require you to serialize the data correctly.
Thanks for reporting this, I'll make sure to update the docs.
Please let me know if there is anything that I can clarify.",{},CONTRIBUTOR,User,False

10834,IssueCommentEvent,2018-02-16 01:35:15+00:00,103969019,aws/aws-xray-sdk-python,3795229,haotianw465,2232217,aws,296884120,366120022,"The PR looks good on most part. But I would like to point out some concerns on the structure of the subsegments this patching code is generating.

In a steady state one http outbound call is broken into two subsegments. One is for `pre_req` and one is for `getresponse`. These two are siblings with the same name (the url without query string). The service graph statistics will be impacted on both call rate and avg latency on that node.
Ideally in a successfully case there should be only one subsegment representing one complete round trip. In a failure case there is one subsegment representing the failed connection.

I understand the ideal case could be a challenge given this is a low level library and a logical operation is broken into several functions. But on the X-Ray service graph level one endpoint should be abstracted to one node with correct statistics.
Another question is that you are saying one http call will generate three subsegments? From the PR I can only see two.",{},CONTRIBUTOR,User,False

11081,IssueCommentEvent,2016-04-15 10:23:32+00:00,16316451,GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python,112928,jgeewax,2810941,GoogleCloudPlatform,148073942,210404328,@rimey, just tossing out ideas. Not sure if they're any good.
> I would be wary of trying too hard to define a one-size-fits-all CRUD pattern.

This is a guideline for the common case that we keep seeing. Cloud Storage, Pub/Sub, etc all have basic CRUD operations and static resource names. We do different things for these similar scenarios and I don't think we should.
This isn't meant to be commandments stating that all APIs must look exactly like this. If they can look this way, they should. If we have a good reason to deviate, we should.
> client.thing(id).get() forces the client library to tolerate partly constructed objects.
I'm fine with dropping the `client.thing(id).get()` pattern generally in favor of `client.get_thing(id)`, however no matter what we do I think we need to support ""partially constructed objects"".

The API might require something by the time it arrives, however our client needs to accommodate people who don't have all of the information at init-time. Otherwise, we're just encouraging people to fill in dummy values until they have all the information they need. Sanjay G is a big proponent of ""tell users quickly when they supply bad data"", and even in Protobuf we don't deal with required fields this way.
In other words, I don't think `__init__()` methods should use parameters without defaults as a way of conveying that certain fields are required.
> Informing the user of the usage error through an exception raised by save() is not optimal.

If a field has to be a number and a user gives us ""foo"", we should tell them right away (protobuf does this). If they make a `Thing` object and so far haven't set the `id` field (which is required), any sort of exception at this point seems premature.

In other words, users say ""I'm done!"" by calling an API-contacting method (`.get()`, `.save()`, etc). At that point, it makes sense to say ""hey... you didn't provide an ID... and to save this you have to pick one..."".

As I mentioned above, this isn't a commandment. In Datastore, the ID field is ""required"" to be a persisted entity, but it's fine to omit the ID when saving a new one -- that's how you say ""give me an auto-generated ID"" -- so this certainly isn't a one-size-fits-all mandate.",{},,User,False

11211,IssueCommentEvent,2016-11-29 04:39:14+00:00,16316451,GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python,520669,dhermes,2810941,GoogleCloudPlatform,189298462,263474300,"> We're going to need a way to test all the examples, not just those embedded in docstrings: do you have a plan for that?

Yes, though that is ""beyond"" the scope of this PR. There are two possible approaches that I can see. The first is to ""guess"" which hand-written RST files apply to a given package (I am not ""pro"" on this approach). The second is as follows:

-   Discontinue handwritten RST files and use `sphinx-apidoc` to generate our docs (I wrote the docs gen for `oauth2client` using this and @jonparrott ""copied"" that approach in https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-auth-library-python/)
- In this world, our ""package usage"" docs will go in `google/cloud/foo/__init__.py`
-   Add checks that literal blocks are as we expect, e.g. no python literal blocks that aren't tested
> Memory eludes me: what stops us from just running `sphinx-build -b doctest` in place in the normal `docs` directory, rather than synthesizing one?
The synthesized one is an attempt to isolate on a per-package basis (just as is done with the system tests). It's not strictly necessary.
The ""real"" reason I did it was because our docs build takes quite a long time (since the project is so large), and I wanted to have a snappier doctest build.",{},,User,False

11352,IssueCommentEvent,2018-03-24 08:49:21+00:00,4086616,shadowsocks/shadowsocks,17123090,studentmain,3006190,shadowsocks,304705320,375856980,"Hello World <notifications@github.com> 于 2018年3月24日周六 16:20写道：
> 这项目麻烦早就大了去了
> 我觉得你的逻辑很奇怪。正因为这样会有麻烦，所以利用GitHub的邮件功能互相私下交换下信息就好了，不要让自己暴露在这里。当然，有些人可能不够聪明，为了追求社交上的存在感，豁出去了。
> 我个人觉得现在还在写翻墙软件的人，无非两种：要么为了名声，要么为了冲Star。这两样跟被请喝茶相比都不值。不如把自己的小软件写好，让自己和自己的朋友们没有后顾之忧或是有个后路。
> 毕竟，哪怕你无私风险，利他主义的分享了出来。之后你又能得到什么呢？写一份保证书送一杯茶？
> 不知道有没有人想过当时SS作者被喝茶的时候心理活动是怎样的。看看SS项目给作者带去了什么：荣耀么？收入么？从他被喝茶之后写的感想来看，恐怕是麻烦和恐惧居多吧？
> 你看看现在这几个仓库的Issues，每个月有几个有价值的？大部分都是使用问题，比“怎么不能用了？”稍微好一点而已。你真愿意写出一个项目给那些人用么？如果是真的的话，那么你也太不了解你的用户了。
> 前段时间吧，某个人趁着SS大范围不稳定，在这推了自己的工具叫lightsocks，被众人指出根本不懂密码学。然而呢，现在那个工具有一千多个Star。那些Star那个项目的人，未来有可能成为你的用户，想想吧。
> 所以，如果你真的聪明，就不要在这里留痕迹，因为这里根本不值得冒险。看到这，如果各位觉得有道理，就把上面引用我的邮件删除吧。我不想给自己惹麻烦。
> You are receiving this because you were mentioned.
> Reply to this email directly, view it on GitHub
心态是好的，能力有待提高。
没有冒犯的意思，但是建议你先学一学密码学再来写这种工具，因为在没有相关知识的前提下写出来的代码实在危险。
> 在这半年里重来没有出现过问题
这句话完全不能说明任何问题。
另外，你的实现跟Shadowsocks相比完全没有优势的地方，如果Shadowsocks能被识别，你的也很快就会被识别。而且仅仅封装了Socks5的Connect请求，功能性上也不如Shadowsocks。
最后， 这[两行代码](https://github.com/gwuhaolin/lightsocks/blob/e2ee0fbf1c79b5dcbd5b9ea76321eb38be68a9c7/server/server.go#L57)怎么可能能实现注视中所说明的：
你连listener都没有关闭啊！而且当然你关不掉，因为`listener`这个变量一直在`Listen`接收器里，没有导出。其他函数根本碰不到它。
所以就现在的代码而言，你还需要一个新连接来Unblock [Accept循环](https://github.com/gwuhaolin/lightsocks/blob/e2ee0fbf1c79b5dcbd5b9ea76321eb38be68a9c7/server/server.go#L44)，然后才能读到`server.running`这个变量，退掉循环，关掉服务器。
事实上，你目前是让Golang运行时来响应SIGINT，然后让操作系统来回收那些资源。不知道你这半年有没有发现这个问题？
Golang里正确关掉服务器TCP的方法是这样的：

1. 建立Acceptor循环的时候，确保当Accept失败时会读取一个变量`running`，当`running` == `false`的时候退出Acceptor循环，其他情况等待一小段时间重试Accept（因为Accept会返回其他错误，比如Too many file descriptor）。就像这样：`if acceptErr != nil { if !running { break } time.Sleep(1 * time.Second); continue } `，只是你可能不需要等1秒这么久；
3. 关掉Listener，这时候处于阻塞状态的Accept函数会立即失败并且返回Broken Pipe，然后读到`running`变量退出循环；
4. 关掉所有的客户端连接。",{},NONE,User,False

11551,IssueCommentEvent,2018-01-12 04:11:53+00:00,7209075,pantsbuild/pants,1883523,kwlzn,3065172,pantsbuild,279594278,357138578,"to be clear: this goes deeper than just echoing - currently in the daemon, things like up/down/left/right/^D in the repl don't work as expected either because those are all functions of the terminal driver. I'd also expect e.g. a `./pants run` that launched a full blown curses app to not work either.

I think longer term, the v2-y solution here may be to directly execv a built product (an external binary, test or repl entrypoint) directly from the thin client as console task (effectively removing the middle man - this would be the cleanest approach).
the interim solution needs more consideration. something along the lines of:
- write/emulate a small terminal driver w/ ""good enough"" settings and/or something controllable (e.g. via contextmanager to wrap repl/run with config like 'echo off', etc).
- make the nailgun protocol aware of tty settings and send delta events on setting changes (if those changes are even recognized/discoverable by the pty pair via normal e.g. `stty` means)?

- some other UNIX-y way of connecting the pty on the pantsd-runner and tty of the thin client (`setpgid` may help at least for signaling..). or maybe some way to open/read/write directly form the tty as file (`/dev/...`).",{},MEMBER,User,False

11609,IssueCommentEvent,2018-08-01 19:10:46+00:00,7209075,pantsbuild/pants,46740,stuhood,3065172,pantsbuild,346342050,409688876,"There is one failing integration test to act as a reminder that this represents a major behavior change.
...individual targets are able to claim to be compatible with `CPython>3` while depending on targets with no declared compatibility, and things will just work.

After this patch, the default compatibility for a repository would be specified via the constraints, and so rather than being completely unconstrained, a target with no compatibility argument would pick up the default. In the case above, that would cause a failure.
How do we feel about this? There are a few potential deployment strategies for a mixed repository:
1. don't pin interpreters in pants.ini, and only pin compatibility on binaries, or on targets where it is relevant
2. pin interpreters in pants.ini, but for a range wide enough to cover all of your binary's/tests compatibilities
3. pin interpreters for a ""default"" compatibility (ie, default to 2), and then explicitly clear the default on libraries to make them ""agnostic"": ie, `python_library(.., compatibility=[])`.

No, it actually uses its own mapping class based on the underlying XML tree data. The order is therefore preserved.
> lxml, FWIW, explicitly documents everywhere that the order is ""arbitrary"".

Arbitrary"" in the sense that it depends on where the attributes came from. If from the parser, then it's document order. If added later, it might make a difference whether the attribute already existed before, but AFAIR it still gets appended at the end. It's up to libxml2, and lxml doesn't give any guarantees here on its own. It's unlikely that the current behaviour of libxml2 is ever going to change, though, as it is considered a feature.",{},,User,False

For shared namespaces, we're trying to tackle this for device groups on Panorama and for Vsys on Firewalls.  Both have a shared namespace that acts similarly.  We spent some time discussing design options here and came up with something similar to your SharedDeviceGroup, but a little more generic and reusable.  It would be a new kind of object which I don't have a name for yet but is best described as a 'container object' because it contains other objects.  So your config tree would have a Panorama object at the root, DeviceGroups as children, and a container object called 'Shared'.  The DeviceGroups and Shared container could all have AddressObjects and other policy objects.  That same Shared object is reusable in that it could be used on a firewall as the shared namespace for Vsys.  It would act mostly like any other PanObject but would not itself generate XML or XPaths, delegating that task to its children instead.
So in the end, it would behave basically the same as what you have here in this PR, but different implementation under the hood.
Let me know what you think.  Hoping to get some time to work on this soon, I think it could be a really useful addition to PanDevice as a whole.",{},,User,False

The use of `repr` just to ensure that Py2 and Py3 output an edge case looks a bit like a hack to me. What I mean is, when we're all on Py3 XXX years from now, it's not going to matter and someone updating the validator is going to have to pause to figure out why `rep` is in there. As far as the 10 digit limitation goes, I did that on purpose. Not every environment is going to have the same level of precision. Besides, we're making fonts on a pretty coarse grid. 12501,IssueCommentEvent,2016-04-22 00:08:19+00:00,32382669,crossbario/txaio,145599,meejah,5656948,crossbario,149974960,213167214,"So, in the current API I'm proposing, it sounds pretty similar to that: there's ``make_batched_timer`` that returns instances of an internal-only helper that happens to conform to an interface. See: https://github.com/crossbario/txaio/pull/67/files#diff-a5e5b34d1134c66bcfe8aa3ec80dac8fR27
Reasons for introducing ``IBatchedTimer`` as an interface are:
- we can't use Twisted interfaces directly, as txaio can be used *without* installing Twisted at all

- make it more-clear we're not providing ""full"" Twisted API (e.g. ``IDelayedCall`` provides more than just ``.cancel`` on the returned objects, but the ""lowest common denominator"" here is that the ""thing that ``call_later()`` returns"" can only have ``.cancel()``)

- txaio (for better or for worse) provides a module-level ``call_later`` API (instead of, e.g., trying to invent a ""common"" reactor/event-loop interface class) and has a ""global"" configured event-loop/reactor. It might be ""better"" to abstract the reactor/event-loop such that you can use txaio over multiple loops etc. ... but, it doesn't. Pragmatically: Twisted doesn't support multiple reactors (yet) anyway, and Autobahn doesn't need this.

So to summarize, before this change txaio had one API: ``txaio.call_later``. This change introduces a single method (``txaio.make_batched_timer``) which has just a ``.call_later`` method, with the same API as ``txaio.call_later`` (returns ""a thing which has a ``.cancel``, and schedules a call for later).",{},,User,False

> Excel treats all numbers as floats. In general, it doesn't care whether your_number % 1 == 0.0 is true or not.
> xlrd is giving you all your numbers as floats, and your booleans as integers. How you print them out is purely a python matter https://stackoverflow.com/a/22245269/6056362
Plus I played around with both libraries and can just confirm above.
As a conclusion, I can say that think it's fine that both libraries are parsing any number as float and we should leave as is.

But on the other hand, parsing booleans as integers (not even strings `""1""` or `""0""`) are really weird behaviour IMO. As a solution, we can check cell type and if it's `XL_CELL_BOOLEAN` than just `bool(cell_value)`.  probably between this two lines https://github.com/frictionlessdata/tabulator-py/blob/master/tabulator/parsers/xls.py#L89-L90

12795,IssueCommentEvent,2017-12-04 15:57:52+00:00,53344710,globus/globus-sdk-python,1300022,sirosen,6120321,globus,266904416,349005903,"Okay, Py3.3 has been EOLed as of a couple of months ago: https://www.python.org/downloads/release/python-337/
@jaswilli, should we maybe drop _official_ Py3.3 support, make `[jwt]` included by default, and close the books on this until someone comes back to us with an issue?

A more sophisticated option: because we only ship source distributions, we can have setuptools dispatch in its behavior based on the environment. We could keep the dependency optional, have it installed by default, and then add a var like `GLOBUS_SDK_SETUP_SKIP_JWT=1`. We'd keep all of our skippable tests and skip the install of pyjwt in our test matrix for at least one tested version.

`pip` and `setuptools` also let us dispatch on platform, so we could check for Windows + Py3.3 explicitly (but what if it breaks on 3.7 or starts breaking on 2.7 or 3.4 or... There are so many ways for it to go wrong.",{},OWNER,User,False

13141,IssueCommentEvent,2015-04-15 20:13:44+00:00,16557526,pysam-developers/pysam,4426336,AndreasHeger,6598771,pysam-developers,68742173,93552768,"Thanks. None is returned because the read is marked as unmapped (flag=20). I am not exactly sure why I added this test - I guess it made sense at the time. I will remove it.

The current proxy classes and properties are pretty fast, in spite of the overhead involved in creating Python objects for keys and values every time they are accessed.  Do you have an example where the overhead is substantial relative to any real processing done in Python?  If so, the next simple optimization would be to cache Python string versions of chrom/info/format string keys.  Although easy enough to implement, I've put it off intentionally because it is possible for other non-pysam-aware C or Cython code to modify header objects under the hood.  This can lead to loss of coherence between the C and Python objects and could result in incorrect results or segmentation faults.

Full per-instance caches are certainly possible, but can also suffer from the same coherence problems and greatly increase the memory footprint of variant objects.  Unless cache management was extremely explicit, the extra storage overhead could easily make materializing modestly large sets of variants prohibitively expensive (say a few 10s of thousands).",{},,User,False

13188,IssueCommentEvent,2018-05-06 19:02:45+00:00,32769228,cockroachdb/cockroachdb-python,160562,bdarnell,6748139,cockroachdb,319141417,386904459,"OK, I found it: you're using the JSON type, not JSONB. The magic is here:

When has_native_json is true, we don't call into the result processor or json_deserializer. In the cockroach dialect, we set `_has_native_jsonb` but not `_has_native_json` (even though we support both names for the same type, and it just works if we set `_has_native_json`.

So in the short term you can work around this by using `sqlalchemy.dialects.postgres.JSONB` instead of `JSON`, and in a future version of `cockroachdb-python` we'll set the `_has_native_json` flag too. ",{},OWNER,User,False

13263,IssueCommentEvent,2016-12-01 04:48:05+00:00,51040886,Azure/azure-cli,3630974,djyou,6844498,Azure,192693519,264078149,"Client id and secret would be the right terminology. We don't have a preference for our commands, but the inconsistency is from the fact that `client_id` is not a property of service principal or application object as returned by the server and written in the spec. `create-for-rbac` is a special command that does application creation, service principal creation, and role assignment all together. The user, however, will not be able to see the terminology `client_id` or `client_secret` from any other commands in `az ad app` and `az ad sp`. Our commands could live either way, the burden is on the user to figure out which one of `appId` or `objectId` or some other property is in fact `client_id`.",{},,User,False
