> Powered by [Codecov](https://codecov.io/gh/Azure/azure-cli/pull/4047?src=pr&el=footer). Last update [dd91433...18754a5](https://codecov.io/gh/Azure/azure-cli/pull/4047?src=pr&el=lastupdated). Read the [comment docs](https://docs.codecov.io/docs/pull-request-comments).
Here is summary of answer to your questions:

1. --allow-updates: we don't want to change the description to ""specify to ..."", this string is directly copied from python SDK and come from swagger spec. We removed the ""specify to ..."" string from a property of swagger spec since C#/Java SDK will automatic add ""Gets or sets... "" to the beginning of the property title string. Right now, the properties title in our swagger spec all starts with just noun instead of verb.
2. ""dispaly-name"" is just a FYI string, if you don't specify it, it just return none.

3. --version is like a id, it can be anything string (not necessary as version). This is bad naming of our swagger spec (the root cause is our RP API). We don't want to do anything from our cli unless the RP side change the string.
4. we will add the enum_choice_list to ""thumbprint-algorithm""
5. we will add the enum_choice_list(Type) to possible enum parameters, also fix the os-family list
6. The only string fit into ARM id category is ""storage-account-id"", which I already changed to ""storage-account"" parameter.

7. we only add prefix to parameter when we find there are conflict between 2 parameter. For example, both ""job-manager-task"" and ""job-prepartion-task"" have property named ""id"", to flatten them in the command line parameter, we rename them to ""job-manager-task-id"" and ""job-prepartion-task-id"", otherwise, the parameter names are exactly same as properties name.
8. we will do more investigate to reduce the level of flatten to reduce the parameter number.

,{},,User,False

13347,IssueCommentEvent,2017-04-12 22:26:26+00:00,51040886,Azure/azure-cli,6014209,yugangw-msft,6844498,Azure,220292463,293725016,"@msleal, @colemickens, I am fixing this bug. Do apologize for the inconvenience.
> the file .azure/accessTokens.json holds the credentials in clear

We cache the SP secret, because the token issued for SP carries no refresh token. It is not reasonable for CLI to prompt you for creds every one hour, and actually it is not even possible as the token can expire in the middle of the command session.

CLI sets the file permission at 600, meaning other users will not be able to read it. I can't say the plain file has no security risk but it is still reasonably secure . Also we don't persist user's creds at all.

,{},,User,False

For 3, This is about the cli configuration management, related but a different thing.

For 4, Good point, but let us track it separately, particular per user voices. The global configuration, due to the nature and impact, is confusion prone. 13851,IssueCommentEvent,2015-12-14 10:35:38+00:00,33653601,jupyter/notebook,327925,takluyver,7388996,jupyter,121916500,164404736,"The [IPEP for this](https://github.com/ipython/ipython/wiki/IPEP-25%3A-Registry-of-installed-kernels#kernel-directories) originally specified the same rules as Python 2 identifiers - ascii letters, numbers and underscore, and can't start with a number. It doesn't look like that got translated into docs or code, though.

The current regex will match any Unicode word character on Python 3, as well as `_-/.`. I think we should make that more specific to ascii characters (`[a-zA-Z0-9_\-\.]`), to avoid any potential confusion relating to filesystem encoding. I don't particularly mind which bits of punctuation we support, but it should all be ascii, and I'd rather avoid anything that has significance in URLs, like `?` or `%`.",{},,User,False

14069,IssueCommentEvent,2017-09-18 15:51:14+00:00,27082457,HIPS/autograd,1458824,mattjj,7935606,HIPS,258498107,330266890,"We should probably separately time the `autograd.misc.flatten._flatten` function. I'm not sure what to compare it to, but we could ensure that it's not the cause of the slowdown.

It looks like the slowdown is probably from the slick implementation in terms of make_vjp. It's possible that if we make make_vjp faster (which we already intend to do), this gap will close, at least to where the implementations are in the same OOM (it's not good when µs goes to ms). If we can't close the gap between the nice implementation and the more ""manual"" version, we should probably go back to it. The silver lining is that looking into the details of unflatten might help us make faster VJP functions.

One final thought: should we make these benchmarks bigger, so that they look more like flattening a list/dict of neural network parameters? If this overhead is purely additive, then maybe it's not such a big deal. @dougalm has said before that all benchmarks should report the time as T = a + b N, where N is some measure of input size. (Maybe more generally we should specify the asymptotic order of complexity of a polynomial-time algorithm and the benchmark could estimate the monomial coefficients up to that order...)",{},CONTRIBUTOR,User,False

14086,IssueCommentEvent,2017-02-27 21:08:09+00:00,27082457,HIPS/autograd,1458824,mattjj,7935606,HIPS,210568400,282853707,"Wow good point about memoizing everywhere! I think within a single make_vjp function, getitem would be called with the same argument values more often than other functions. However it might not be any cheaper to hash indices than ndarray addresses; that is, while we could further reduce node duplication by memoizing all functions on the values of their ndarray arguments, we could cover a common case by just memoizing on the ndarray addresses, and that doesn't require reading the whole array. (But maybe we can get away with only reading part of the array, since this is only an optimization.)

I'd also say that changing SequenceNode (or the sequence_take primitive) is a lot easier than changing all functions, and I have a use case for it now. Maybe a vague argument for it being special is that tuples are somehow special: sometimes we rewrite function signatures to take one tuple argument instead multiple arguments (like we do in multigrad, which is a good model for my use case), yet it can incur a lot of node duplication overhead. Same goes for primitives that package up ""multiple return values"" in tuples: I don't like those causing overheads.",{},,User,False

I proposed that because usually `pytest-qt` creates the `QApplication` object inside the `qapp` fixture:

If you don't want to rely on the fixture being present (makes sense because the user might not be using `pytest-qt` at all) notice that `qapp` doesn't try to create the `QApplication` again if it has been created already, so it is possible for someone else to create `QApplication` and install the reactor.

The `pytest_twisted_install_reactor` hook idea seems nice, but we still would have problems if twisted installs reactors just by importing some module; then users and pytest-twisted own test suite will need to take extra care to not import things at the module level if they want to use different reactors.

The point that I misunderstood is that the cases au = al = (ub - lb) / 2 are not the typical cases. The equality au (or al) = (ub - lb) / 2 only when ub and lb have the same sign and |ub - lb| < (1 + |lb| (or |ub|)) / 10 hold. In short, al = (1 + |lb|)/20 and au = (1 + |ub|) / 20 is the typical choice and then about 90% of the feasible interval is linearly mapped.

14783,IssueCommentEvent,2017-02-15 21:18:39+00:00,56217288,confluentinc/confluent-kafka-python,12688620,mayurjpatel,9439498,confluentinc,191471304,280141954,"I ran into this leak today as well. I created a test producer program that sends 20k messages to a kafka topic and waits for it to be flushed. Running the test program under a debug python build and valgrind helped me pinpoint the leak.
==14139== 479,928 bytes in 19,997 blocks are definitely lost in loss record 517 of 518
It looks like `msgstate` object in `Producer_produce` call is never getting freed up in success case. Calling `Producer_msgstate_destroy` just before returning seems to eliminate the leak.

`msgstate` is a struct of 3 pointers. On a 64-bit machine, that translates to a leak of 24 bytes per successful produce. This matches the valgrind report of 479,928 bytes lost over 19,997 blocks (479,928/19,997 == 24).",{},,User,False

15174,IssueCommentEvent,2018-03-06 05:16:55+00:00,112676803,apple/turicreate,902935,gustavla,10639145,apple,302168784,370665864,"Hi @johnyquest7, I thought I would add some more detailed info. All vision toolkits (classifier/similarity/detector) perform implicit resizing to get the data into a fixed size expected by the underlying model. For instance, the ResNet-50 backend for the classifier takes a 224x224 image, so if you provide an image of size 500x400, it will be automatically resized to 224x224 before passed to the model. It may violate the aspect ratio doing this, but the classifier networks are pretty robust to this kind of deformation. There is no cropping involved.

This is what happens at test time for all three models and during training for classifier/similarity. The object detector during training on the other hand does extensive data augmentation, which means each time an image is presented to the algorithm (each image repeated many times) will be randomly resized, cropped, translated, color perturbed, and mirror flipped.",{},COLLABORATOR,User,False

We actually run analysis on the evm bytecode vs solidity, to map an instruction to a piece of code we use solc. If the whole piece of code is shown, that means that solc mapped that instruction to the entire code. I think your problem (multiple seemingly duplicate issues) is probably caused by false positives (i.e. mythril observes some weird behavior, assumes it is wrong and reports it, while it is actually intended by the compiler). 15553,IssueCommentEvent,2016-02-22 01:26:30+00:00,32944298,Z3Prover/z3,3085284,NikolajBjorner,11668813,Z3Prover,135286419,186961069,It seems that your tool, CBMC, creates a text-pipe that sends SMT-LIB2 instructions to Z3.

You can pose queries to Z3 using formulas that use quantifiers and if the formulas are satisfiable, you can use the satisfying model to evaluate ground expressions. Evaluating quantified expressions under a model is still as hard as checking satisfiability, so a simple evaluation engine would not work.
So Z3 chooses not to support evaluating quantified formulas.
There is probably something that the CBMC pipe can do differently to handle cases like these.
For example, if it wants to know the truth value of a quantified formula F, it can create a definition
(assert (= q F)) using a predicate symbol q and then work using q. A model for constraints that include F would evaluate F to the same truth value as q.
Could you follow up with CBMC on how to resolve this issue? It isn't really a Z3 bug, and we will not be supporting evaluation of quantified formulas. ",{},,User,False

15870,IssueCommentEvent,2015-10-23 03:22:52+00:00,27790033,blaze/odo,829044,darwindave,12833921,blaze,112740230,150447692,"Quickest fix seemed to be by running the following in linux before.

2. yeah -- I saw checkers only as an addition for that limited set of cases, suchas 'environment', which would include e.g. information about infrastructure itself (see #55). So they are invoked ones in a runtime whenever any `duecredit_cite` command to be invoked

3. Cool.  So that then should be the next one on our table to tackle -- decide on the format.  I think we should have smth like `.duecredit/citations/` subdirectory where we would collect `{octave,python}.json` citations, which then would all be picked up by `summary` command.  We also might end up with `.duecredit/config` which then could be used to override defaults (e.g. format to output stuff in etc)

4.1. ah btw -- to ease adoption... That is why we came up with that https://github.com/duecredit/duecredit/blob/master/duecredit/stub.py which would be a minimal thing to include into projects to provide necessary API but so that if duecredit is not installed -- their stuff still works as usual.  I guess in case of the matlab/octave implementation if overloading names would be tricky, stub file could define those proxy `duecredit_cite` whenever actual, bigger, duecredit module define `duecredit_cite_` to be called by those stubbed ones

4.2. In this Python implementation we have separated `Entry` (Doi, BibTex, etc) from actual `Citation`.  Entries could be loaded from anywhere -- .bib file or specified directly in the code.  Then `Citation` could either consume a new Entry or just a key identifying preloaded one + accept usage tags and description for it.  This way a single bibliographic reference could be used for multiple citations in different places, possibly with different description/path/etc.  So we kinda supported all a b c I guess ;)  But in majority of usecases we seems to use only c atm.

5.  How we thought is:  by default ducredit should have no impact/affect on anything, and only the run with 'DUECREDIT_ENABLE' environment variable -- it starts tracking.  Then 'summary' is independent of that -- it just loads up stored citations, filters by desired tag, and presents them.  But may be we could/should enable it by default -- not sure, since with all the injections it does have some measurable impact on imports time ATM.",{},,User,False

Re: Damping. We used regular damping not factored damping in the code. We will fix it in the paper.
Re: Architecture. For A2C it's the regular DQN architecture, 32-64-64-512-x. For ACKTR, we changed the last conv layer to 32 for speeding up matrix inversion, 32-64-32-512-x.

Re: A2C performance. I'd be very surprised to see if A2C can as good as ACKTR, which also implies algorithms like PPO or ACER do not help either. The results we put in the paper are standard results trained using baselines code; it's shared across multiple publications (PPO, Equivalence Between Policy Gradients and Soft Q-Learning). I'd be very curious to know if you have some better hyperparameters. ",{},NONE,User,False

But, if you change the current default order, you can and will break some builds.

16725,IssueCommentEvent,2017-09-01 17:44:27+00:00,55808787,openthread/openthread,4882339,GiedriusM,16142269,openthread,254606443,326642456,"> otJoinerStart() and otCommissionerAddJoiner() take the PSKd (i.e. the binary form).
I was pretty sure that these functions take Joining Device Credential (JDC). There are few reasons for that:
1) Thread 1.1.1 spec, section 8.4.4.5 mentions JDC instead of PSKd

```The Commissioner, upon reception of a DTLS-ClientHello message with a cookie from one of its scanned Joiners, inspects the Joiner IID, and looks up the Joining Device Credential to use to continue the DTLS handshake for that Joiner.```
2) `otJoinerStart` and all other related functions use `const char *aPKSd` type with no length indication (`strlen(aPSKd)` is used), which does not allow a null-character inside of a PKSd.
3) JDC has a maximum length of 32 symbols and given the base32 (2^5), the maximum binary (base-256) length would be 32*5/8=160bits or 20 bytes. However PSKd in OpenThread is `kPskMaxLength = 32`.
1) Should the OpenThread's joiner API take JDC string (and convert it internally) or a pointer to PSKd byte array and it's length as the parameters?
2) Is the Thread spec misleading and the maximum PSKd length is 32 bytes and 32*8/5=51.2 bytes for the JDC.

3) Is there a base32-thread encoding format reference? For example, what is the padding symbol? Or is the PSKd length limited to multiples of 40 bits, so that it could be expressed with whole-bytes in both base-32 and base-256 (binary) formats.",{},CONTRIBUTOR,User,False

17143,IssueCommentEvent,2017-06-28 13:05:05+00:00,38581626,pybind/pybind11,578702,jdumas,17565521,pybind,238947997,311653748,"Well, I don't understand why you can't make it work the way it is right now (i.e. calling `find_package(PythonInterp)` before `find_package(PythonLibsNew)` should work no matter what).

The vhost that you've included is on Port 80. Certbot will happily copy that vhost and include a reference to the certificate that you have for bavaria.domain.net if you select `1: Attempt to reinstall this existing certificate`.

When you select `2: Renew & replace the cert (limit ~5 per 7 days)` certbot assumes that you've already installed this certificate and goes and looks for the vhost file that it would have created. 18029,IssueCommentEvent,2017-08-07 20:47:30+00:00,68376693,pybel/pybel,5069736,cthoyt,22892058,pybel,248388013,320776115,"I've searched the internet for solutions to installing multiple packages with the same name, but haven't found anything good. It's not a very common problem in the Python community since the standard is for packages to have the same name on PyPI as the name under which they install.

I've read your paper and documentation and it seems to me like `pybel` is a subordinate of `openbabel`. In other large software ecosystems like `sqlalchemy`, subordinate packages that are standard get installed to `sqlalchemy.ext.contrib`. Alternatively, `flask` took a different approach (which we also took with extensions to our `pybel`) where extensions are named `flask_admin` and so on.

If I were to use your package (and after looking through it I will be searching for an opportunity to do so!) then I think I might also be confused as to why there is another package imported in examples, and then try to install `pybel` from pip myself. A solution might be to change your setup.py to install the current `pybel` to something like `openbabel_pybel` or even better, restructure your project so `pybel` is a subpackage of `openbabel` and can be imported as `openbabel.pybel`. This would make it 100% explicit (and Tim Peters would be proud)

In the mean time, we can post a notice on the readme for our package with a link to your installation documentation and a short explanation of the problem for any users who are confused. I hope this is agreeable for now.

,{},,User,False

18229,IssueCommentEvent,2017-11-27 23:34:04+00:00,79083614,python-trio/trio,609896,njsmith,26335827,python-trio,202426973,347364727,"> If the other tasks also called `uasyncio.sleep(0)`, then they got rescheduled at the same time unit (millisecond for uasyncio) and before the ""unlucky"" task(s). As an example, if we scheduled tasks 1, 2, 3, 4, then only tasks 1, 2, 3 had a chance to run, and 4 was always dangling at the end of queue and barely had a chance to run.

Ah, right, that kind of task starvation is definitely bad. Curio also had a number of issues like this before I fixed them. Trio avoids this by dequeuing tasks in batches: first it collects up *everything* that's ready to run *right now*, then it runs all those, and then only after it's finished that batch does it go back and look for more things to run. So if tasks 1, 2, 3 reschedule themselves immediately, that's fine, they get put in the queue, but we don't look at that queue until after we've finished running task 4 and go back to create our next batch. And the randomization happens *within* each batch, so it can't create any kind of long-term unfairness – at the boundaries between batches, every task has (deterministically!) run exactly as many times as it would have with strict FIFO.
Well, now I feel even sillier for having missed uasyncio before! It's definitely exciting to find others exploring the space of asyncio alternatives...",{},OWNER,User,False

18231,IssueCommentEvent,2017-11-06 02:08:41+00:00,79083614,python-trio/trio,33187,nmalaguti,26335827,python-trio,271281731,342027967,"I think monkey patching on import makes it hard to opt out of the behavior. I don't think the behavior is bad or dangerous, but I can see it being irritating trying to avoid (we could rename the original so someone could put it back).

Ideally users would be able to do something similar to how django sets up its loggers, where they have specific handlers for everything under the `django` namespace. We could advocate something similar for trio that uses a specific formatter for those logs (`trio.LoggingFormatter`).

If we're looking to make it easy for people who just want it to work, offering a `trio.monkey.patch_logging()` that does the monkey patch seems like a reasonable compromise. You need to do slightly more than just import trio, but if you are already logging in the first place you probably have a config where adding `trio.LoggingFormatter` isn't a big deal.
If you're already using something like https://github.com/madzak/python-json-logger, then monkey patching really is your best option at that point.",{},CONTRIBUTOR,User,False

18248,IssueCommentEvent,2017-03-13 21:48:13+00:00,79083614,python-trio/trio,609896,njsmith,26335827,python-trio,213446762,286254422,"> I envisioned this as a debug-mode (or call it ""development mode"") only check. In production, turn the debug mode off and buckle up.

A debug mode would be better than nothing, but if possible I'd really prefer something that we can leave on in general. Imagine if functions in ""production mode"" responded to getting the wrong number of arguments by silently returning some weird object instead of raising an error – it's kind of the same thing. And debug modes impose a substantial cognitive overhead – you have to document them, every beginner has to remember to turn them on 100% of the time, some of them will fail or forget and we're back to getting confusing errors, etc.
> Now this is pretty ugly and will make asyncio code slower under PyPy ;) Frankly, I don't think we can implement any revision of the `__acall__` idea at this point.

Conceptually the `f_inawait` and `__acall__` proposals are very similar: in both cases we're passing an extra bit of information down to the coroutine object (""is this call in `await` context, true/false""), and then it can respond to that bit however. The `__acall__` method of passing this information seems more explicit and consistent with how we pass information around in Python normally, that's all...

Re: PyPy, we're talking about a simple wrapper object that has no state and one method that unconditionally calls another method. This is the sort of thing that their JIT eats for breakfast :-). On CPython it's worse because you can't optimize out the allocation of the wrapper object. But if this is a serious problem then instead of a wrapper it could be a flag that we set on the coroutine object (sorta similar to how `@types.coroutine` works – it could return a wrapper but for efficiency it doesn't). Or we could have a global flag instead, and have async functions do something like:
raise TypeError(""async function called without await"")

I just thought it was a bit nicer to re-use `set_coroutine_wrapper` rather than introduce a new global flag :-). (Also, for all of these approaches we have flexibility about the default – we could just as easily make it trio/curio that have to set some special flag or whatever we go with.) But in general my point is that the ""how do we configure the policy async functions use to handle being called outside `await` context"" part is orthogonal to the ""how do async functions tell whether they are in `await` context or not"" part.",{},,User,False

I think there should generally be 2 levels.  The first level (e.g. sbp, hsm, and integ currently) defines what the package is and protects the user from name conflicts with other code.  The second level (if necessary) should be a details namespace to hide stuff that you don't want the user to pull into global scope if they do something like using namespace sbp.
In particular, if a user is using Rachel's moments code, they should make an OBJECT_DATA object with

That's a lot of namespace stuff without any real usefulness as far as I can tell.  Now, maybe this hierarchy is important for getting the python interface working correctly, in which case I'll defer.
